questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: This item asks for an underlying assumption. The author claims that the situation involving alcohol limits and it is unlike that which existed with respect to alcohol during prohibition. What is the difference? According to the author, heroin and cocaine are not preferred by the middle-class. Thus, we can infer (though the author never specifically says so), that alcohol, even during prohibition, was preferred by the middle-class.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: This item asks you to identify hidden assumptions in the argument. The author states that if not an assumption of the argument, for the author need not commit himself to the view that children accept every idea. It is just that they would be more critical of ideas if given philosophical training. The author is, however, committed to II. If children were not able to grasp philosophical concepts, then it would make no sense trying to teach them the concepts. Also, the author is committed to III. Implicit in the author's parent is the premise that it is a good idea to have students learn new ideas - otherwise, the author would be content to let them absorb only these without question.

questions.body: This argument is, in fact, a kind of generalisation. It assumes that what is true of one magazine is true of other magazines as well. (C) points out that the argument is implicitly committed to this idea.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: The assumption is that potential donors will be flattered by requests for large donations and frequently offended by requests for smaller amounts. Therefore, it is worth the gamble to start high. At worse, the potential donor may decrease his gift. Alternatives (A), (B) and (D) are not assumptions made in the statement. Alternative (E) is partially correct; donors are seldom offended if they are asked too much by fund-raisers.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: If the principal monetary policy is to be attained - reducing the import surplus while resuming economic growth - per capita consumption will have to be frozen. Thus, consumers will have to be persuaded to give up consumption to further national economic goals. The assumption is that people will be willing to put a halt to growth in their standard of living.
